coredata.dc:title: Facile development of carbon quantum dot deposited titanate-based materials for environmental application
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen-Le, Minh-Tri
Nguyen, Cuu Khoa
Nam, Pham Hong
Thoa, Dao Thi
Phong, Nguyen Van
Tri, Nguyen Huu
Viet, Nguyen Minh
Viet Ha, Tran Thi
Nguyen, Luu Huu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we reported a facile EDTA-Na2 assisted method to synthesize CQD deposited titanate based materials for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. A variety of characterization methods (XRD, FTIR, DRS, Raman, UV–Vis, BET, etc.) were used to study the characteristics of the obtained materials. The results showed that the CQD deposited titanate based materials had stronger visible light absorption than the pristine titanate-based materials. Moreover, they also exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of MB into CO2. More than 90% of MB was quickly removed by one of these materials after 90-min reaction which obeyed the first-order kinetic model. .OH, .O2 − and h+ were dominant active species for the MB photodegradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanate
Carbon quantum dot
Methylene blue
Photocatalytic
EDTA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and morphology of CQDs
Optical properties and morphologies of the titanate-based material

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by Graduate University of Science and Technology under grant number GUST.STS.ĐT2019-KHVL04 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of a) S0, b) S1, c) S2 and d) S3.
Raman spectra of the pristine TiO2 and the titanate-based material.
a) UV–Vis spectra of the CQDs synthesized at different time intervals of hydrothermal process. (The inset: the pictures of the CQD solutions). b) TEM image of CQD-8h.
FTIR spectra of S0, S1, S2 and S3.
FTIR spectra of CQD@S0, S1, S2 and S3.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) CQD@S0, S1, S2 and S3. (b) S0 and S3.
SEM images of S0 (a) and S3 (c). EDX analysis of S0 (b) and S3 (d).
a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light. b) Mineralization of MB to CO2 during the photodegradation.

Kinetic study of the MB photodegradation: (a) First-order kinetic reaction model; (b) Determination of the reaction rate constant; (c) Recycling photoactivity tests of CQD@S3 for the degradation of MB.
The MB photodegradation in the presence of different scavengers.
Schematic mechanism of the photocatalytic reactions.
Textural properties of the pristine TiO2 and titanate-based materials.

head.ce:title: Facile development of carbon quantum dot deposited titanate-based materials for environmental application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we reported a facile EDTA-Na2 assisted method to synthesize CQD deposited titanate based materials for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. A variety of characterization methods (XRD, FTIR, DRS, Raman, UV–Vis, BET, etc.) were used to study the characteristics of the obtained materials. The results showed that the CQD deposited titanate based materials had stronger visible light absorption than the pristine titanate-based materials. Moreover, they also exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of MB into CO2. More than 90% of MB was quickly removed by one of these materials after 90-min reaction which obeyed the first-order kinetic model. .OH, .O2 − and h+ were dominant active species for the MB photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:para: The 21st century has witnessed accelerated rate of industrial development which caused rigorous environmental issues resulting in air, water and soil contamination. Particularly, water pollution has been drawn much concern of the public due to its strong impact on our daily life [1]. It was reported that the major source of water pollution is industrial discharge which releases many harmful substances into the aqueous media such as dyes, heavy metals, pharmaceuticals, and a variety of other organic pollutants [2]. Most of chemicals were found to be persistent and non-bidegradable. They may cause detrimental effects on human health and aqueous environment, and are responsible for mutations and physiologic dilemma in living organisms even at low concentration [3]. Typically, cationic dyes such as methylene blue, crystal violet, methyl red have been well known for their carcinogenic, mutagenic nature [4]. Therefore, it is important to develop a method to treat those deleterious chemicals. Several approaches such as chemical deposition, coagulation-flocculation, ion exchange, photocatalytic degradation, membrane separation and adsorption have been proposed to eradicate cationic dyes [5–7]. Unlike other treatment methods, photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes using photocatalysts has gained much attention because of their versatile application and ability to completely remove dyes [8].

From the perspective of engineering strategies, titanium dioxide (TiO2), a non-toxic photocatalyst with outstanding physical and chemical properties such as robust photochemical stability and high biocompatibility, appears to be a distinguished candidate for the photocatalytic purpose [3]. However, the wide band gap energy of TiO2 and its fast charge carrier recombination are the key bottlenecks affecting its solar energy harvesting and photocatalytic efficiency [9]. Till now, much effort, such as fabrication of cocatalysts, metal and non-metal doping, and modulation of structural morphology, etc, has been devoted toward the enhancement of its solar light absorption ability and charge separation [10–14]. Among them, the construction of photocatalyst heterojunctions has demonstrated remarkable suppression of photo-induced charge recombination and boosted the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 [15–17]. More recently, titanates or titania based materials have gained much attention in the field of photocatalytic dye degradation due to their intrinsic high surface area, their superior thermal stability, as well as their moderate photocatalytic activity [18]. Different photocatalytic systems using titanate based heterostructures have been employed for the decolorization of dyes [18]. Sodium titanates are one of titanate based heterostructures. Generally, sodium titanates (Na2TinO2n+1) consist of two common structures: the layered structure (i.e. Na2Ti3O7) and the tunnelled structure (i.e. Na2Ti6O13) [19]. Titanate-TiO2 hybrids have been studied for different advanced applications [20–22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are low cost fluorescent carbon based nanomaterials with outstanding optical properties, remarkable catalytic activity and high biocompatibility. These materials have been coupled with various types of photocatalysts to enhance their light harvesting ability [23–25]. Therefore, it is promising to coupling CQDs with titanate-TiO2 hybrids for dye treatment. Until now, although there have been a number of publications on CQDs [26–32], there is lack of studies about such materials for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes.

Therefore, in this study, we aimed to synthesize CQD deposited titanate based materials for the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue. Kinetic studies of the photocatalytic reaction were also investigated, and the photocatalytic mechanism was also discussed in detail.

The titanate based materials were typically synthesized via an EDTA-Na2 –assisted sol-gel route. Initially, three certain increasing amounts of EDTA-Na2 (99%, Sigma–Aldrich) were dissolved into a solution of 100 mL of deionized water and 1.40 mL of acetic acid. To this solution was a mixture of titanium (IV) isopropoxide (97%, Sigma–Aldrich) and isopropanol added dropwise while conducting vigorous stirring to make a sol solution. After 24 h gelation in dark, the solution was heated at 70 °C for 1 h for solvent evaporation. The solvent-free samples were further placed in a furnace for calcination at 400 °C for 5 h to obtain solid samples which were subsequently ground into fine powders to obtain the final samples denoted as S1, S2 and S3. For comparison, the synthesis of pure TiO2 (hereafter, S0) was conducted using the same process without EDTA-Na2.

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1.0 g of glucose (commercial grade) was dissolved into 100 mL of distilled water stored in a beaker under vigorous stirring at 30 °C for 5 min. The solution was then transferred to a closed 100 mL autoclave. The system was subsequently placed in an oven for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C in different periods of time: 4 h, 6 h and 8 h. After cooling to a room temperature, the obtained dark-brown solutions were passed through a dialysis tube (∼12,000 Da) to remove large particles whose molecular weights are larger than 12,000 Da. Those samples were defined as CQD-4h, CQD-6h and CQD-8h.

To fabricate CQD deposited titanate based materials, 10 mL of CQD sample with the highest visible light absorbance was mixed with 1 g of the synthesized materials under vigorous stirring for 30 min. The mixtures were heated at 80 °C for solvent evaporization until getting dry samples, and the samples were then treated at 400 °C to get final samples denoted as CQD@S0, CQD@S1, CQD@S2 and CQD@S3.

The morphology of the materials was observed on a Scanning Electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with an energy − dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. Crystal phases of the samples were determined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns recorded on a Bruker D8 advance powder diffractometer with CuKα emission, 40 kV/30 mA current and continuous scanning 2θ = 10° - 80°. The Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRam HR micro-Raman instrument with a 532 nm Ar + ion laser at room temperature. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) transmittance spectrawere recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 470 FTIR spectrometer at room temperature. The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of the materials were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the reference material to study the optical properties of the materials. The specific Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore size were measured by N2 adsorption-desorption method using an ASAP 2020 apparatus under nitrogen air. The chemical composition of the materials was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Scientific Sigma instrument under ultra high vacuum (<10−6 Pa) with a monochromatic AlKα source (photon energy 1486.6 eV).

The photocatalytic activity of the materials was evaluated via a photodegradation of MB under visible light. Prior to irradiation with visible light, 20 mg of the synthesized catalysts was dispersed into 20 mL of 10 mg L−1 MB solution placed in a 100 mL closed system using a Pyrex photoreactor. Air was continuously purged into the reaction system with a flow rate of 10 cc/min. The reaction system was placed in dark under vigorous stirring for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was then irradiated with visible light (Philips lamps, 400 nm–800 nm, 14 W, 1 mW cm−2) for 90 min. Aliquots of MB solution were taken out at every interval of 30 min during the reaction, and then centrifuged at 3000 rmp for 5 min. Concentration of MB in the supernatants was measured at 665 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

XRD diffraction of S0 in Fig. 1 a showed the characteristic peaks of the only anatase phase (PDF no. 21–1272) [33], a common phase of TiO2. In contrast, the titanate based materials showed XRD patterns with more than one phase. The XRD pattern of the S1 sample (Fig. 1b) revealed the existence of titanate structures which are the layered titanate Na2Ti3O7 (PDF no. 31–1329) and the tunnel structured titanate Na3Ti6O13 (PDF no. 37–0951) [34]. These two phases were retained in the XRD spectra of S2 and S3 samples (Fig. 1c and d). In addition, another sodium carbonate Na2CO3 phase was also detected in these two XRD spectra [35]. The formation of sodium carbonate species was due to the heat treatment of the samples in the presence of EDTA-Na2. An increase in the EDTA-Na2 amount resulted in increasing the crystalline structure of titanate phases and carbonate species. The results indicated that the introduction of EDTA-Na2 would favor the formation of titanate based structures and sodium carbonate species.

To confirm the phases of the pure TiO2 and titanate-based materials, Raman spectroscopy, a highly sensitive analysis technique, was carried out for S0 and S3 samples. As shown in Fig. 2
, in the case of S0 sample, those peaks at 143, 194, 393, 515 and 636 cm−1 were assigned to the Raman scattering peaks of anatase phase of pristine TiO2 [36–38]. When it comes to S3, a series of peaks around 251, 531, 601 and 684 cm−1 were the Raman active modes related to Na2Ti6O13, while those at 302, 366 and 422 cm−1 belonged to Na2Ti3O7 [39,40]. Moreover, the peaks at 181, 451 and 922 cm−1 can be ascribed to Na–O–Ti, Ti–O–Ti stretching modes of titanates, and Ti–O stretching mode involving non-bridging oxygen atoms, respectively [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and morphology of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The light harvesting ability of CQDs synthesized under the hydrothermal condition at different time intervals was investigated using UV–Vis Spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3a, all CQD samples showed absorption in both UV and visible range. The absorption below 300 nm corresponded to π- π* transition in aromatic sp2 CC carbons of CQDs, while the tailing starting form 300 nm–400 nm and above 400 nm was related to the intrinsic absorption of n- π* transition in the CO carbon core, and CO surface state transition with a lone pair of CQDs, respectively [42]. An increase in the hydrothermal time led to an increase in the light absorbance of CQD solutions and red shifts of their absorption wavelength, probably due to their increasing average size [43]. It is reported that the hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs is a bottom-up approach in which CQDs are produced by the assembly of smaller glucose molecules. Therefore, the long hydrothermal treatment time would facilitate the assembly of small glucose molecules to form larger sized CQDs. This statement was confirmed by investigating the size distribution of the obtained CQDs. It can be deduced from the size distribution histograms (Fig. S1) that the average sizes of the obtained CQDs are in the range of 1.47–22.20 nm, with the size distribution in the range of 1.3–33.0 nm. The longer time of hydrothermal treatment would result in the larger size of CQDs. The optical band gap energies of the as-prepared CQDs obtained from the Kubelka-Munk method were 3.56, 3.23 and 3.15 eV for CQD-4h, CQD-6h and CQD-8h, respectively. Amongst the CQD samples, CQD-8h gave the highest light absorption ability, indicating that 8 h of hydrothermal treatment was suitable to produce CQDs with an optimal light capture ability. The presence of CQDs in the solution after 8 h of hydrothermal treatment was further characterized by TEM (Fig. 3b). It can be clearly observed that CQDs synthesized after 8 h had sizes of nanometer and were well-distributed in the solution. Moreover, the zeta potential of the CQDs solution stored for 1 year was −11 mV (Fig. S2). The negatively charged surface of CQDs could prevent their agglomeration in a certain period of time due to the generation of repulsive force between CQDs. However, its low negative value indicated the poor stability of CQDs, and their visible light absorption ability has changed significantly after storing them for 1 year (Fig. S3). Therefore, it would be better to use CQDs solutions right after their synthesis.

Fig. 4 showed the FTIR spectra of TiO2 and titanate based materials before coupling with CQDs. There were three peaks involved in the FTIR spectra of TiO2, that were, approximately at 3427 cm−1, 1623 cm−1 and 591 cm−1. These peaks could be assigned to the stretching, deforming vibration of O–H bondings in the H2O molecules and stretching vibration of Ti–O–Ti bondings in the TiO6 octahedral structure [44]. When it comes to FTIR spectra of titanate based materials, there were several intensive peaks in addition to those of H2O molecules and TiO2. The intensive peak at 1454 cm−1 was ascribed to characteristic vibration of polydentate carbonaceous species, while the shoulder at 1329 cm−1 could be assigned to bidentate carbonaceous species deposited on the surface of the materials. Moreover, the sharp and less intensive peaks at 881 cm−1 demonstrated Na–O–Ti bondings of sodium titanate [10].

It can be clearly seen from Fig. 5 that the intensity of all characteristic peaks of the as-synthesized materials was remarkably decreased after being coated with CQDs due to the interaction between carbonaceous functional groups of the titanate based materials and CQDs, indicating that CQDs were successfully deposited on the titanate based materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and morphologies of the titanate-based material
ce:section.ce:para: The light absorption ability of the synthesized materials was demonstrated in Fig. 6. No visible light absorption was observed for the pristine TiO2 or titanate-based materials. As compared to the pristine TiO2 and titanate-based materials, all of the CQD deposited titanate-based materials had a significant absorption in the visible range (400 nm–800 nm) (Fig. 6a). Their higher visible light absorption intensity (Fig. 6b) was due to the surface deposition of CQDs which have outstanding optical properties with the strong absorption under visible light. The absorbance of CQDs modified samples was increased with an increase in the amount of EDTA-Na2 modifier, indicating the successful adsorption of CQDs on the surface of titanate based materials at high levels of EDTA-Na2. The band gap energy values of CQD@Sx (x = 0, 1, 2, 3) obtained from the Kubelka-Munk method were 2.71, 2.54, 2.40 and 2.19 eV for CQD@S0, CQD@S1, CQD@S2 and CQD@S3, respectively. The remarkable decrease in the band gap energies of the CQD modified samples indicated the more effective incorporation of CQDs into the structural lattice of TiO2 and titanate based materials. Amongst CQD modified materials, CQD@S3 showed the highest absorption in the visible region as well as the narrowest band gap energy. Its band gap energy was remarkably lower than that of its pristine form (S3), which was determined as 3.92 eV. Similarly, the band gap energy of CQD modified TiO2 (CQD@S0) was relatively lower than that of its pristine form (S0), which was about 3.18 eV. The results revealed that the deposition of CQDs on the surface of TiO2 and titanate base materials resulted in the enhancement in the visible light absorption of those materials as well as the red shifts in their band edges. It is because the fact that the incorporation of CQDs introduced mid-gap energy levels to the band gap of those materials. These phenomena were useful to enhance photocatalytic activity of the materials under visible light.

The morphologies of S0 and S3 were depicted in Fig. 7a and c, respectively. The pristine TiO2 (S0) exhibited a rock-like morphology made of clusters of agglomerated particles of various sizes. Similar observation was also true for the titanate-based material (S3). The difference between them lies in their elemental composition. S0 was consisted of three elements, while S3 was consisted of four elements (Fig. 7b and d). The presence of the sodium (Na) element in S3 was due to the formation of Na2Ti S3O7, Na2Ti6O13 and Na2CO3. The chemical composition of S0 and S3 was further confirmed by XPS spectra (Fig. S4). The elemental composition of S0 detected by EDX were also observed in its XPS spectra. In contrast, nitrogen which could not be detected by EDX was observed in the XPS spectra of S3 and accounted for 0.59 at.%.

The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 (S0) and titanate based materials (S1-3) before and after the deposition of CQDs were investigated through the degradation of MB under the visible light irradiation. Before the photocatalytic activity test, all samples were checked for the adsorption equilibrium. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that the pristine TiO2 (S0) showed no MB adsorption after 30 min of the dark reaction, while the titanate based materials showed about 10%–20% of MB removed by the adsorption. The higher MB adsorption ability of titanate-based materials was due to their higher surface areas and the presence of the carbonaceous species which had an affinity with MB molecules through an electron donor-acceptor interaction between highly electronegative O atoms of carbonaceous species and nitrogen acceptor sites of MB molecules. After the treatment with CQDs, both CQD@S0 and CQD@Sx (x = 1, 2, 3) exhibited higher MB adsorption ability. They, except for CQD@S1, showed a further remarkable decolorization of MB. A saturated MB removal of CQD@S1 under the visible light irradiation can be a result of its large portion of surfacial adsorbed MB which prevented it from the light penetration. It can be noted that although the titanate-based materials had no ability to absorb the visible light, their MB removal was remarkable due to the fact that small portion of MB adsorbed on their surface could act as a dye sensitizer, leading to photosensitized degradation of MB on their surfaces [45]. This phenomena were not observed for the pristine TiO2 (S0) due to no adsorption of MB taken place. Amongst the as-synthesized samples, CQD@S3 had the highest ability to remove MB from the solution due to its enhanced photocatalytic activity as a result of its strong visible light absorption, narrow band gap and remarkable dye adsorption. More than 90% of MB was removed by CQD@S3 after 90 min reactions.

To study kinetics of the reaction, the first order kinetic model (ln C = -kt + lnCo, where Co and C were the concentration of MB at initial and time t, k was the reaction rate constant) was applied to the photocatalytic decomposition of MB under visible light. It can be seen from Fig. 9 a that the reactions over the studied materials showed the best fit to the first-oder kinetic model. The reaction rate constants, k, were then calculated as 0.57 × 10−3 min−1, 4.31 × 10−3 min−1, 3.48 × 10−3 min−1, 2.29 × 10−3 min−1, 5.37 × 10−3, 0.53 × 10−3, 4.23 × 10−3 and 32.4 × 10−3 for S0, S1, S2, S3, CQD@S0, CQD@S1, CQD@S2 and CQD@S3, respectively. Amongst the as-synthesized materials, CQD@S3 revealed the fastest photodegradation of MB.

The recycling photoactivity test of CQD@S3 was carried out to evaluate its photostability under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 9c, the photocatalytic activity could still be reasonable with only 4.4% decrease of the dye photodegradation efficiency, which indicated that CQD@S3 had high photostability during the photocatalysis.

To check the mineralization of MB to CO2 as using CQD@S3, the outlet gas stream of the gas-closed photoreactor was passed through a CO2 analyser (Series 9610-Alphal Omega Instruments) connected with the photoreactor during the photodegradation. The concentration (C) of CO2 was recorded at every interval of 30 min during the reaction. As seen in Fig. 8b, the release of CO2 from the photodegradation reached a peak after 30 min of photodegradation and was declined afterwards. The results indicated that CQD@S3 had an ability to completely mineralize MB to CO2 during the photodegradation.

In an attempt to elucidate the mechanism involved in the photodegradation of MB, t-butyl alcohol (TBA), benzoquinone (BQ) and EDTA-Na 2 were added to the photocatalytic system as scavengers of .OH, .O2 − and h+ species. As shown in Fig. 10 , the addition of those scavengers hindered the photodegradation, leading to the decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency. The results indicates that .OH, .O2 − and h+ were dominant active species for the MB photodegradation.

Fig. 11 illustrates the proposed photocatalytic mechanism of the MB photodegradation. There were several synergistic factors contributing to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQD deposited titanate based materials. Firstly, effective adsorption of MB on the CQD deposited titanate based materials was a prerequisite for improving the photodegradation of the pollutant. The MB could absorb the visible light to produce excited states (denoted as S*) due to its self-photosensitization, and the excited electrons could also inject to the photocatalyst system from the photosensitive MB. Secondly, the low band gap energy and strong visible light absorption of the materials would facilitate the formation of photo-induced charge carriers such as electrons and holes, which will improve the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. Thirdly, CQDs can act as electron donors which are capable of inserting electrons into the conduction band of sodium titanate Na2Ti3O7 or Na2Ti6O13 [26–32]. Moreover, the up-conversion of CQDs can convert longer wavelength light to shorter wavelength light, which can in turn excite titanate to form photoexcited electrons and holes.

In summary, we successfully fabricated the CQD deposited titanate based composites via an EDTA-Na2 –assisted sol-gel route. As compared to the materials without CQDs, the composites exhibited narrower band gap and stronger absorption in the visible light region. These properties were really useful for the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation. The presence of photosensitizers and effective electron-hole separation made CQD modified composites have higher photocatalytic activity in the MB removal than the unmodified materials. .OH, .O2 − and h+ were dominant active species for the MB photodegradation.
This research is funded by Graduate University of Science and Technology under grant number GUST.STS.ĐT2019-KHVL04.
Not applicable.
This manuscript does not contain any studies with animals performed by any of the authors.
This manuscript does not contain any studies with human participants performed by any of the authors.
This article has the consent of all the authors and authors have no conflicts of interest.
All data related to this publication are made available in the article.

Minh-Tri Nguyen-Le: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing. Cuu Khoa Nguyen: Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Pham Hong Nam: Formal analysis, Investigation. Dao Thi Thoa: Formal analysis. Nguyen Van Phong: Investigation. Nguyen Huu Tri: Investigation. Nguyen Minh Viet: Investigation. Tran Thi Viet Ha: Investigation. Luu Huu Nguyen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Luu Huu Nguyen is thankful to Van Lang University. Pham Hong Nam is thankful to Institute of Materials Science, Vietnam Academy of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adsorption of cationic dyes onto carrageenan and itaconic acid-based superabsorbent hydrogel: synthesis, characterization and isotherm analysis
J. Hazard Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sharma, G. Sharma, A. Kumar, T.S. AlGarni, M. Naushad, Z.A. Alothman, F.J.
